champion.narrative: Vincent Weber (Purchasing Manager, 16+ years tenure, responsible for digitalization, savings plan, and responsible procurement) is the single-threaded champion. He initiated contact in 2024-2025, re-engaged Pierre Meraud on 16 Mar 2026 after the board diagnostic stalled the project, and drove the 16 Apr demo. He has strong access to the economic case (he owns the ROI brief and will present it internally) and to the CPO (Céline Braibant, his direct or dotted-line manager, who will present the final business case to the board). Evidence of advocacy: Vincent explicitly told Pierre on 16 Mar 'it's interesting that you reached back out because the project will relaunch soon' and 'I think we will re-include you once we relaunch,' signaling intent to bring Zycus back into the shortlist. He provided detailed pain narrative, asked for revised pricing, case studies, and consulting contacts to build the internal business case, and scheduled the 16 Apr demo—all champion behaviors. On 16 Apr he expressed being 'pleasantly surprised by Zycus improvements since two years ago,' a positive sentiment shift. He confirmed 'CPO Vincent Weber confirmed Zycus is in the loop' (Next_Step 20 Apr; note the confusing phrasing—Vincent Weber is Purchasing Manager, not CPO; likely the note conflates Vincent's statement that the CPO has confirmed Zycus's inclusion). Current engagement: last direct touchpoint was 16 Apr demo (71 days ago); last Salesforce activity was rep-initiated follow-up 15 Jun per Next_Step ('chase Vincent to see if they moved forward on budget validation'). No buyer reply recorded since 15 Jun, so engagement is cooling but not cold—Vincent is awaiting board decision and has nothing new to report until that gate clears. Champion strength assessment: developing. Vincent has project ownership, internal credibility (16 years tenure), access to the CPO, and advocacy intent, BUT he lacks budget authority (he is not the EB) and cannot unblock the current stall (board prioritization is above his paygrade). He has not multi-threaded us to the board-level decision maker, and he has not formalized Zycus as the preferred vendor (we are 'in the loop' but competing against Ariba and others). At-risk factors: (1) long silence (71 days since last buyer touchpoint), (2) competing priorities (IT projects, other transformation initiatives may crowd out procurement digitalization), (3) competitor Ariba mentioned as having 'challenges with responsiveness and contact continuity' but still in the mix, (4) prior Zycus pricing rejection creates residual skepticism (Pierre committed to revised pricing but has not yet sent the formal updated offer per 16 Mar commitment). Recommended move: re-engage Vincent with the promised artifacts (revised pricing, case studies, consulting contacts, Google Chat integration timeline, iSave follow-up) and ask for a board-decision readout—has the project been greenlit, and if so, what is the vendor selection timeline? If the board has not decided, ask if an executive briefing (Pierre + the deal owner's manager to Céline Braibant or the CFO) would help tip the prioritization decision in procurement's favor. Vincent is a solid champion but cannot close the deal alone; we need executive sponsorship to convert his advocacy into budget and priority.

competition.narrative: The competitive field is partially disclosed. SAP Ariba is the named incumbent peer and the strongest current threat. Vincent Weber stated on 16 Mar 2026: 'Competitor solutions like Ariba face challenges in responsiveness and continuity of contacts... every three months we have a new contact with them, it's quite complicated.' This positions Ariba as active in the 2024-2025 evaluation cycle but struggling on relationship and execution—a known weakness Zycus can exploit with continuity (Pierre as single point of contact) and responsiveness. Vincent also mentioned 'we had a few presentations from other solutions that gave us quite a bit of satisfaction' (16 Mar), confirming at least one other unnamed vendor in the shortlist, but no name was provided. The prior Zycus offer (late 2024/early 2025, delivered by predecessor Renaud Leroy) was 'completely beyond what Saverglass was prepared to pay and light-years from what competitors proposed,' leading to Zycus being eliminated on price. Pierre committed on 16 Mar to revised pricing 'X percent below Renaud's offer, suited to mid-sized companies,' repositioning Zycus as cost-competitive. Current status: as of 20 Apr Next_Step, 'Zycus is in the loop' (back in consideration post-pricing revision and demo). The deal is a 3+ horse race (Zycus, Ariba, at least one other), not yet down-selected. Threat assessment: Ariba is high threat (SAP installed base, brand, P2P heritage) but exposed on service/relationship and potentially on price/fit for mid-market. The unnamed competitor(s) are medium threat (satisfied Vincent in prior demos but not named or detailed). Zycus's wedge: (1) revised mid-market pricing vs. original offer and vs. Ariba, (2) modern UX and AI (Merlin Intake, Vincent was 'pleasantly surprised' on 16 Apr), (3) low IT burden (Saverglass's top concern; Zycus SAP integration is standard, middleware-friendly), (4) Google Workspace roadmap (Ariba likely weaker here; Zycus confirmed Google Chat integration coming in next few months), (5) relationship continuity (Pierre as stable contact vs. Ariba's churn). How we win: deliver on the 16 Mar commitments (revised pricing, case studies, consulting contacts, IT resource scoping, Google Chat timeline) to prove Zycus is now the right-sized, right-priced, right-fit solution; position Pierre + Brice (solutions consultant) + product/Merlin team as a stable, responsive, mid-market-focused alternative to Ariba's enterprise-motion service gaps; co-build the board-facing ROI model with Vincent to make Zycus the path of least resistance once budget is approved; and secure executive sponsorship (Pierre + the deal owner's manager brief Céline or the CFO) to tip board prioritization toward procurement digitalization with Zycus as the vendor. The competitive window is open—we are back in the mix—but vendor selection has not started because budget approval is pending.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is well-documented and acute, owned by Vincent Weber (Purchasing Manager, procurement digitalization lead). Three primary pain categories: (1) FTE capacity exhaustion—same headcount, exploding compliance workload (RSE questionnaires for 100% of new suppliers, legal doc control for all active suppliers, risk committee reviews); Vincent stated 'tasks we used to do well, we can't do anymore... we are losing time and can't keep up' (16 Mar). (2) Contract risk and financial leakage—manual contract management with no termination alerts has caused Saverglass to miss renewal deadlines on 'three or four contracts' and continue paying for unwanted services; Vincent cited this as the simplest ROI proof ('take the history of contracts we forgot to terminate and kept paying—that alone justifies the solution'). (3) Audit and compliance risk—Saverglass recently underwent LRQA audit; existing manual supplier evaluation tools (a small in-house module called 'star performance' and point solution Approval for RSE/legal scoring) were flagged as unreliable; Vincent confirmed 'we need to pass suppliers through risk and compliance committee and today we lose enormous time and some things fall through the cracks' (16 Mar). Audit readiness is a board-level concern. Secondary pains: (4) IT resource bottleneck—IT team stretched by e-invoicing reform (France Jan 2025, Belgium, UAE 2026), SAP upgrade, and competing transformation projects; procurement digitalization was delayed in 2024-2025 due to IT prioritization of invoicing over SRM/CLM. Vincent emphasized 'IT resource availability is a major concern for project implementation' and asked for low-IT-burden integration approach. (5) Rogue spend and request chaos—procurement requests come via email, phone, ad-hoc platform, and chat with no central intake; lack of consolidated channel causes 'rogue spend and user frustration due to unclear policies' (16 Apr demo notes). (6) Complex capex tender management—high volume of multi-lot RFPs on short timelines with heavy admin burden and no tooling beyond the in-house Cap 6 (limited to email drafting, comparison tables, contract template assist). Pain owner: Vincent Weber is the operational pain owner (he runs digitalization, feels the FTE crunch, presents the business case); Céline Braibant (CPO) is the executive pain owner (accountable to the board for procurement performance and audit readiness). Pain is confirmed, recent (audit was recent, contract misses are historical but recurring risk, IT bottleneck is live), and tied to measurable business impact (financial leakage, audit findings, FTE overload). This is a genuine, urgent pain—the stall is not due to lack of pain but to competing-project prioritization and budget-approval process, which is a timing/process issue, not a qualification issue.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is clear and buyer-controlled, with a well-defined gate sequence: (1) Board diagnostic and prioritization (in progress since Dec 2025, nearing completion as of 16 Mar call)—external cabinet is consolidating all transformation projects (procurement, HR, finance, sales admin, IT) and will present prioritized roadmap to the board in the coming weeks; board will greenlight which projects proceed and in what order. (2) Budget validation and project approval (current gate, blocking forward progress)—Vincent must secure board sign-off on final budget and project scope; Next_Step 15 Jun and 20 Apr confirm this gate is still open ('final budget and project have not been validated'). (3) Vendor selection and RFP/comparative evaluation (not yet started)—once board approves, Vincent will re-engage the shortlist (Zycus, Ariba, and at least one other unnamed competitor from 2024-2025 cycle); Vincent mentioned 'you will need to charm the main purchasing managers' and implied a formal presentation/demo round. (4) Business case and ROI finalization—Vincent and procurement leadership (likely Céline Braibant, CPO) will build the board-facing ROI model anchored to contract-overpayment history, FTE trade-off, and audit risk; Zycus was asked to provide case studies and reference contacts to support this. (5) IT feasibility and resource scoping—IT team (stretched by e-invoicing reform, SAP upgrade, UAE e-invoicing rollout) must confirm integration effort is within capacity; Vincent specifically asked for IT resource requirements and middleware/SAP integration details. (6) Final commercial negotiation and pricing sign-off—previous Zycus offer was rejected on price; revised pricing (Pierre committed to 'X percent below Renaud's offer, much more reasonable') must be validated. (7) Contract and phased rollout plan—Vincent wants a multi-year, multi-module engagement with staggered implementation to spread budget and IT load. Key process insights from the call sequence: Vincent Weber (Purchasing Manager, digitalization lead) is the single-threaded project driver and technical evaluator; he has been in role 16+ years and carries the procurement transformation brief. Céline Braibant (CPO, contact role Decision Maker) was not on either call but is referenced as the presenting executive ('Céline will present all procurement projects to the board'). No CFO, CIO, or CEO has appeared. Pierre Meraud (Zycus rep, owner of this opp) joined mid-cycle (predecessor Renaud Leroy handled 2024-2025 initial contact and pricing); Pierre rebuilt relationship on 16 Mar and delivered revised demo 16 Apr. The process is currently stalled at gate 2 (budget approval) due to competing IT priorities (e-invoicing France/Belgium/UAE, SAP upgrade) and board-led transformation prioritization, not due to vendor evaluation or Zycus performance. Vincent explicitly stated 'I can't tell you the timeline; I'll know more in a month or two' (16 Mar). The 20 Apr Next_Step ('project should be prioritized for 27, very positive feedback following our third presentation') suggests three Zycus touchpoints have occurred (likely initial call with Renaud 2024, Pierre's 16 Mar reboot, Pierre's 16 Apr demo) and that the target deployment is 2027, not 2026. The Dec 2026 CloseDate in Salesforce is a placeholder and should slip to align with 2027 deployment. Recommended move: get Vincent's read on board decision timing (next 30-60 days?) and ask who presents the procurement business case to the board—that is the EB and the path to unblocking the stall.

decision_criteria.narrative: Saverglass's evaluation criteria are well-defined and confirmed across both calls. Primary requirements (stated 16 Mar, validated 16 Apr): (1) SRM—supplier onboarding/offboarding automation, RSE risk scoring and compliance monitoring (Ecovadis integration mentioned), legal documentation control with expiry alerts (URSSAF 6-month renewal is a specific pain point), supplier database cleaning and SAP synchronization. (2) CLM—contract lifecycle management including tender management (RFP/RFQ comparative tables), contract drafting assistance, automated termination/renewal alerts, audit trail, and e-signature (DocuSign mentioned). (3) Minimal IT resource consumption—Vincent stressed 'IT resource availability is a major concern; we need a solution with limited IT involvement due to shortages' and asked for details on middleware integration with the relevant stakeholder and standard Zycus integration patterns to minimize internal IT team drag. (4) Google Workspace compatibility—Saverglass operates on Google Meet/Chat/Docs; Vincent explicitly asked for Google Chat integration timeline for Merlin Intake (Zycus confirmed Teams now, Google Chat within next few months). (5) User-friendliness and modern UX—Vincent noted 'solutions have improved significantly in two years' and expressed being 'pleasantly surprised' by the 16 Apr demo vs. his last view. (6) Economic ROI and board-approvable business case—top management approval hinges on documented savings (contract overpayment avoidance, FTE capacity, audit risk mitigation). (7) Mid-market pricing—Vincent was explicit that the prior Zycus offer (Renaud, late 2024/early 2025) was 'completely beyond what Saverglass was prepared to pay and light-years from what competitors proposed,' leading to Zycus being dropped; Pierre Meraud (new rep) committed on 16 Mar to revised pricing 'much more reasonable, suited to mid-sized companies' and confirmed new strategy targeting €500M–€1B firms. (8) Phased rollout option—Vincent wants to spread budget and IT load over two years ('engage on multiple modules but stagger implementation over two years'). (9) Reference customers and consulting support—Vincent requested 'two or three company contacts that worked with similar industrial structures' and case studies for mid-market ROI validation. Secondary: iSave savings tracking module (follow-up requested 16 Apr), AI-driven intake (Merlin), and integration with Approval (incumbent for RSE/legal scoring, which Saverglass will retain). All criteria are confirmed, not inferred. Zycus's positioning is strong on SRM/CLM/AI/ease-of-integration; the wedge is modern UX, Merlin AI intake, low IT burden, and revised mid-market pricing vs. Ariba (seen as unresponsive, high contact churn per 16 Mar) and vs. prior Zycus offer (too expensive).

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI/Merlin positioning is moderately strong and a confirmed differentiator vs. competitors. Vincent Weber requested Google Chat integration for Merlin Intake on 16 Apr demo, confirming interest in AI-driven procurement request consolidation to reduce rogue spend and improve policy compliance. Demo notes highlight Merlin Intake AI chatbot as a key feature ('embed in Google Chat, Teams, or Slack... enforces policies in real time to eliminate rogue spending'). Products field in Salesforce lists 'Merlin' and 'Merlin Intake' as in-scope modules, so AI is part of the proposed solution. However, AI appetite is not quantified (no AIS score) and AI was not the lead pain driver on the 16 Mar intro call—Vincent led with FTE capacity exhaustion, contract risk/financial leakage, and audit readiness; AI intake was positioned as a 'nice to have' UX improvement and rogue-spend control, not a transformational must-have. No AI-specific ROI case or urgency was articulated. Competitor Ariba likely has weaker AI/modern-UX story (Vincent's criticism was service/relationship, not product features, but his 'pleasantly surprised by Zycus improvements in two years' comment suggests Zycus's AI/UX is now a positive differentiator). Under-positioned risk: moderate. AI is on the table and valued, but not yet the centerpiece of the business case or the forcing function for urgency. If Zycus wants to make AI/Merlin a wedge, the recommended move is to tie Merlin Intake (Google Chat-embedded AI agent) and Merlin negotiation/contract-clause AI to the specific FTE-capacity and audit-readiness pains Vincent articulated—show that AI automates the RSE questionnaire workflow, the supplier onboarding approval chain, and the contract-termination alert that caused the financial leakage. Make AI the 'how' that solves Vincent's 'why' (FTE overload, audit risk, contract misses). Currently AI is a feature demo, not a strategic narrative.
